input:
Disasters rarely begin at the moment of impact. They begin when a system gets used to explaining away what it should have stopped to understand.

Challenger was a long sequence of accommodations, each one easier to defend than the whole chain would have been.

The smoke at T plus zero point six seven eight seconds is why the story should not start with the explosion. The first visible warning was small. Then it vanished. For a while, everything looked normal.

The same thing had already happened inside the organization.

A warning appeared. The system explained it. The launch continued.

That is why the next case belongs beside Challenger. Therac-25 is not another space story. It is a smaller room, a medical machine, a software interface, and operators trying to explain errors the machine was not built to make visible. The form changes. The pattern does not: confidence moves faster than verification.

Next time: Therac-25, a medical machine that exposed what happens when software confidence outruns the evidence users can see.

elevenlabs_previous_text:
The tenth time, it starts to sound like how the system works.

These behaviors appear wherever the cost of stopping is immediate and the cost of continuing is statistical. Healthcare systems. Financial systems. Infrastructure. Software. Any organization that has to decide whether a warning is strong enough to interrupt momentum.

Challenger did not invent this pattern. It made the pattern visible.
